body_txt: Human beings are social creatures and conditioned to respond in certain ways to social niceties. Oftentimes, these pre-programmed responses can slip by our conscious attention, resulting in us agreeing to something we hadn't intended to. Politeness Judo is the art of taking advantage of simple courtesy exchanges to get one's way. Note that this will often fail if the target has No Social Skills and doesn't respond with the appropriate formula . In comedy series and family sitcoms, it's not uncommon for a parent, friend, or love interest to vehemently object to helping the protagonist with something she wants to know or do. The Straight Man is usually being contrary because he's looking out for his own interests; though he might rationalize it as him "knowing better" or to help the protagonist, it's only rarely because what's asked is a truly bad idea . He'll rail and boast that nothing will change his mind, and there's no way that the protagonist will get his permission, help, or the information she wants out of him. The protagonist asks one simple question, cordially phrased and without shouting... and the Straight Man agrees nonchalantly. Usually he'll do a Double Take and realize he said yes offhand. He either forgot his original outrageously contrarian position, got thrown off by the polite tone, or was holding out for that "please". Someone who is Silk Hiding Steel will likely be skilled in this. See also: Duck Season, Rabbit Season ; Passive-Aggressive Kombat , Minnesota Nice , Japanese Politeness , and Compliment Fishing . Not to be confused with Verbal Judo or Outhumbling Each Other . Also not to be confused with Sugary Malice , which can look nice on the surface while being anything but. Can be a way of avoiding or negating It's Not About the Request , where one of the reasons why a person might refuse an order or request is because of the rude way they were asked. May overlap with You Were Trying Too Hard , when a character tries too hard to get something and only has to politely ask for it to succeed. See also Civilized Threat .
